items.q: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'nous'?
items.why: Students apply the same ending across persons. ER verbs use different endings: je parle, tu parles, il parle, NOUS parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. Each subject takes its own ending.
Sensible is a faux ami. French 'sensible' = sensitive/easily affected. English 'sensible' = raisonnable in French. Confusion leads to wrong impressions of someone's character.

items.q: How do you say 'I am eating bread' (some bread) in French?
items.random_wrong: Je mange le pain (which means 'the bread').
items.why: Students drop the partitive. French requires DU/DE LA/DES for 'some' even when English omits it. Skipping it makes the sentence ungrammatical in French.

items.q: Which auxiliary does 'se marier' take in the passé composé?
items.why: It's être. “avoir” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: blended family. “nuclear family” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the PACS introduced in France?
items.why: Exact answer: 1999. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
